medications.current_medications_list:
Albuterol HFA inhaler 90 mcg: 2 puffs every 4–6 hours as needed for wheezing or shortness of breath (typically used a few times per month, more often during spring allergy season).
Fluticasone (Flovent) HFA inhaler 110 mcg: 1 puff twice daily as a maintenance inhaler for mild persistent asthma.
Cetirizine 10 mg tablet: 1 tablet by mouth once daily in the evening for seasonal allergies and chronic sinus congestion.
Sertraline 50 mg tablet: 1 tablet by mouth once daily in the morning for generalized anxiety disorder and mild recurrent depression (started approximately 2021).
Multivitamin (over-the-counter): 1 tablet by mouth once daily with breakfast.
Vitamin D3 2000 IU softgel: 1 softgel by mouth once daily (recommended after slightly low vitamin D level on blood work in 2023).

allergies.drug_allergies_list:
Penicillin and related antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin): developed a widespread itchy rash and hives as a child after taking amoxicillin for an ear infection; no breathing problems at that time, but advised by pediatrician to avoid all penicillin-class antibiotics since then.

No known allergies to sulfa drugs, NSAIDs (ibuprofen/naproxen), or local anesthetics. No known food allergies; mild seasonal environmental allergies (pollen, dust) but those are managed with cetirizine and nasal rinses and are not medication allergies.

additional_health_information.serious_illnesses_or_injuries_explain_li: In 2019 I was involved in a minor car accident (rear-ended at a stop light). I sustained whiplash and a moderate lower back strain but no fractures or head injury. I completed about 8 weeks of physical therapy and fully recovered, though I occasionally get mild low back stiffness after long periods of standing or sitting, which I manage with stretching and exercise.

Aside from that, I have not had any hospitalizations or surgeries. I have mild persistent asthma (diagnosed in my teens) and chronic sinus issues, but these are considered ongoing conditions rather than acute serious illnesses and are well controlled with my current medications and lifestyle changes.
additional_health_information.care_of_physician_explanation_line_1: I see my primary care physician, Dr. Laura McKenzie at Greenfield Family Medicine in Portland, OR, once a year for an annual physical and as needed for follow-ups. She monitors my asthma, seasonal allergies, and mental health (generalized anxiety and mild depression). My asthma is mild and generally triggered by seasonal allergies and upper respiratory infections; we adjusted my inhaler regimen around 2021 to add a daily maintenance inhaler, which has helped reduce flare-ups.
additional_health_information.care_of_physician_explanation_line_2: I also began seeing a therapist and started sertraline 50 mg daily in 2021 for anxiety and low mood related to work stress and the pandemic. The medication has been stable and effective with no major side effects. I have not needed any emergency room visits or hospital stays in the last five years, and routine blood work (including thyroid, liver, kidney function, and blood sugar) has been normal aside from slightly low vitamin D, for which I now take a daily supplement.
